_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Ravi: The city should build a new stadium; it will attract tourists and boost local businesses. Sofia: Public funds should not be spent on a stadium when that money could repair our crumbling schools.
items.Stem: Ravi and Sofia disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: the city should build a new stadium.
items.ChoiceB: public money should ever be spent on any construction.
items.ChoiceE: a new stadium would attract any tourists.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Ravi favors building; Sofia opposes it — the point of disagreement.
items.WhyWrongB: 'Ever...any' is too broad; Sofia objects to this spending, not all construction.
items.WhyWrongC: Only Sofia mentions schools; Ravi does not dispute it.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Ever' overstates; neither makes a universal claim about all stadiums.
items.WhyWrongE: Only Ravi speaks to tourism; Sofia takes no position on it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Dr. Lee: Antibiotics are overprescribed, which accelerates the rise of drug-resistant bacteria. Dr. Nguyen: Withholding antibiotics from patients who need them risks serious complications; doctors must be able to prescribe them freely.
items.Stem: The dialogue most strongly supports the claim that Lee and Nguyen disagree about whether
items.ChoiceA: bacteria are capable of developing resistance.
items.ChoiceE: current antibiotic prescribing should be more restricted.
items.WhyWrongA: This is common ground, not a disagreement.
items.WhyWrongB: Only Nguyen raises this; Lee does not deny it.
items.WhyWrongC: Neither disputes that resistant bacteria are dangerous.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Ever' is too strong; both accept antibiotics are sometimes needed.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Lee wants less prescribing; Nguyen wants free prescribing — a direct clash.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Marcus: Remote work makes teams less productive, because the casual in-person interactions that spark the best ideas are lost. Elena: Studies show remote teams complete more tasks per week than in-office teams, so remote work is more productive.
items.Stem: Marcus and Elena disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: casual interactions can ever spark good ideas.
items.ChoiceC: productivity can be measured in principle.
items.ChoiceD: remote teams complete any tasks at all.
items.WhyWrongA: Only Marcus relies on this; Elena does not deny idea-sparking interactions.
items.WhyWrongB: Out of scope; no one proposes banning office work.
items.WhyWrongC: Neither questions whether productivity is measurable.
items.WhyWrongD: Both accept remote teams complete tasks; not in dispute.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Marcus says remote work lowers productivity; Elena says it raises it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Tom: The museum should charge admission; the revenue is needed to maintain the collection. Wei: Charging admission would keep low-income families away, so the museum should stay free and seek donations instead.
items.Stem: Tom and Wei disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: museums should ever charge for anything at all.
items.ChoiceB: donations are a possible source of funds.
items.ChoiceC: low-income families currently visit the museum often.
items.ChoiceE: the museum needs revenue to maintain its collection.
items.WhyWrongA: 'Ever...anything' is too broad; the dispute is about this museum's admission.
items.WhyWrongB: Wei raises donations and Tom does not deny they are possible.
items.WhyWrongC: Neither states how often such families currently visit.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Tom favors charging admission; Wei opposes it.
items.WhyWrongE: Only Tom speaks to maintenance revenue; Wei does not dispute it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Amara: Standardized tests should be dropped from college admissions; they mainly measure family wealth rather than ability. Ben: Tests give students from little-known schools a way to prove themselves, so admissions should keep them.
items.Stem: Amara and Ben disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: students from little-known schools deserve a fair chance.
items.ChoiceB: standardized test scores correlate with family wealth.
items.ChoiceC: any admissions criterion can be perfectly fair.
items.ChoiceD: colleges should admit any students at all.
items.ChoiceE: colleges should continue to use standardized tests in admissions.
items.WhyWrongA: Both would likely accept this; it is not the clash.
items.WhyWrongB: Only Amara makes this claim; Ben does not address it.
items.WhyWrongD: Too broad; no one questions whether colleges should admit anyone.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Amara wants tests dropped; Ben wants them kept.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Priya: The company's profits fell because it cut its advertising budget. Jon: No — profits fell because a new competitor entered the market; the advertising cuts had nothing to do with it.
items.Stem: Priya and Jon disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: a new competitor entered the market.
items.ChoiceB: the company should increase its advertising budget now.
items.ChoiceD: advertising ever has any effect on profits.
items.ChoiceE: cutting the advertising budget caused the drop in profits.
items.WhyWrongA: Only Jon mentions the competitor; Priya does not dispute it.
items.WhyWrongB: Neither speaker makes a recommendation about future spending.
items.WhyWrongC: Both agree profits fell; that is common ground.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Ever' is too strong; the dispute concerns this particular decline.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Priya blames the ad cuts; Jon says they had no effect.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Nina: We should adopt a four-day workweek; employees will be happier and just as productive as before. Omar: Cramming the same work into four days will exhaust employees and reduce our total output.
items.Stem: Nina and Omar disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: employees would be happier under a four-day workweek.
items.ChoiceB: any change to the workweek is ever worthwhile.
items.ChoiceC: employee happiness matters more than total output.
items.ChoiceD: a four-day workweek would reduce the company's total output.
items.ChoiceE: employees currently work five days each week.
items.WhyWrongA: Only Nina addresses happiness; Omar does not dispute it directly.
items.WhyWrongB: Too broad; the dispute is about this specific change.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Nina says output would hold steady; Omar says it would fall.
items.WhyWrongE: The current schedule is background, not disputed.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Dr. Kim: The city's recent drop in crime is due to our new community-policing program. Dr. Alvarez: Crime fell just as much in neighboring cities that have no such program, so the program is not what caused our decline.
items.Stem: Dr. Kim and Dr. Alvarez disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: the community-policing program caused the drop in crime.
items.ChoiceB: crime in the city has recently dropped.
items.ChoiceD: crime can ever be reduced by any means.
items.ChoiceE: the community-policing program is expensive to operate.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Kim credits the program; Alvarez denies it was the cause.
items.WhyWrongB: Both accept that crime dropped; that is not in dispute.
items.WhyWrongC: Only Alvarez comments on neighboring cities; Kim does not dispute it.
items.WhyWrongD: Neither questions whether crime can be reduced in general.
items.WhyWrongE: Cost is never discussed by either speaker.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Lin: Schools should require every student to learn a musical instrument; it improves their math skills. Raj: School hours are limited, and time spent on music is time taken away from core subjects like math itself.
items.Stem: Lin and Raj disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: requiring instrument instruction would benefit students' academic performance.
items.ChoiceB: all arts programs should be cut from schools.
items.ChoiceC: playing an instrument is enjoyable for students.
items.ChoiceE: mathematics is an important school subject.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Lin says required music helps academics; Raj says it detracts from them.
items.WhyWrongB: Too broad; no one proposes cutting all arts programs.
items.WhyWrongD: Both accept that hours are limited; it is not the clash.
items.WhyWrongE: Both treat math as important; that is common ground.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Farmer A: Organic methods produce healthier soil, so all farms should switch to organic practices. Farmer B: Organic methods yield far less per acre, and feeding a growing population requires the higher output of conventional farming.
items.Stem: Farmer A and Farmer B disagree over whether
items.ChoiceB: organic methods improve the health of the soil.
items.ChoiceC: farming is necessary for society at all.
items.ChoiceD: all farms should switch to organic practices.
items.ChoiceE: organic methods yield less per acre than conventional ones.
items.WhyWrongA: Both accept that the population is growing.
items.WhyWrongB: Only Farmer A asserts this; Farmer B does not dispute soil health.
items.WhyWrongC: Neither questions whether farming is necessary.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Farmer A urges a universal switch; Farmer B argues against it.
items.WhyWrongE: Only Farmer B raises yields; Farmer A takes no stance on them.
